segments.s547-g9: چنین گفت is a formulaic opening meaning “thus he said”; چنین (adverb) -> “thus/so” precedes گفت (past of گفتن, ‘said’). کای is a vocative marker (O), addressing the following noun; نامداران شهر is a genitive/nominal phrase “the famed/renowned ones of the city” (نامداران = notable/renowned people, شهر = city) placed after the vocative.
segments.s547-g10: ز رای و خرد literally “from/opinion and wisdom” = “in counsel and wisdom”; ز is the poetic preposition ‘from/in regard to’, رای = counsel/opinion, و = and, خرد = wisdom. هرک دارید بهر is an embedded relative/imperative-like phrase: هرک (هر که) = “whoever (who)”, دارید = 2nd person plural present of داشتن ‘you have/possess’, and بهر here (= برای) means “for / to offer”; together: “whoever among you has any counsel or wisdom to offer.”
segments.s547-g11: بدانید is an imperative (2nd plural) of دانستن used as “know / be aware”; کاین = که + این (that this) introduces the clause describing the subject. تیرگردان سپهر is a poetic compound epithet: تیرگردان (“arrow‑turner,” i.e., archer/one who directs arrows) + سپهر (“the sky/heavens”), a personification of Fate/heaven acting as the subject.
segments.s547-g12: This clause uses two negative‑mood predicates about the subject. ننازد is the negative 3rd‑person present form (ن + نازد) — here poetic/archaic — conveying “does not esteem / does not concern itself with”; به داد = “toward/with justice” (داد = justice). و links the two predicates; نیازد (again 3rd‑person present) here means “needs / requires,” so نِیازد = “does not need”; به مهر = “to/for affection/kindness.” The negative prefix on ننازد/نیازد negates each verb for the heavenly agent.
segments.s547-g13: یکی را is the indefinite object “someone” with the object marker را. چو here = چو + present verb carries the temporal sense “when/whenever”; خواهد is 3rd‑person singular present of خواستن used as ‘wills/pleases’ (it wishes); برآرد is a present/subjunctive/optative‑flavored 3rd‑person form meaning “raises up / brings up”; بلند is an adverb/adjective ‘high/lofty’ describing how he raises the person.
segments.s547-g14: هم آخر here links and frames the concluding action — هم as a connective (“also/and then”) and آخر = “at the end / finally.” سپارد is 3rd‑person singular present of سپردن “to consign/entrust” (here “consigns/leaves”); به خاک = “to the earth/dust”; نژند is an adjective ‘base/worthless’ describing the dust. The sequence shows a contrastive turn: exaltation followed by final humiliation.
segments.s547-g15: نماند is a negative‑sense present meaning “does not remain / is not left” (here used impersonally: nothing remains). به جز = “except for”; نام = “name/reputation” (i.e., fame). ز و در جهان is a poetic ordering: ز here functions as a preposition ‘of/from’ or marker linking ‘نام’ to ‘در جهان’ — the sense: “in the world.” Together: “Nothing remains in the world except his name.”
segments.s547-g16: همه رنج = “all suffering” (همه modifying رنج). با او شود = “will come upon/befall him” (با = with/upon, او = him, شود = 3rd‑person singular of شدن used as future/impersonal ‘will happen’). در نهان = “in secret / secretly.” The clause states that all his pains will occur privately, despite his public name remaining.
segments.s547-g1: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. از marks origin 'from'. روم، چین، ترک، هند are place names listed in a series; وز is a poetic contraction (و + ز) meaning 'and from' (the provided sense list does not include this poetic usage). Word order is paratactic: 'چو از X وز Y وز Z و W' = 'when from X and from Y and from Z and W'.
segments.s547-g2: جهان شد is a light-predicate 'the world became / was (filled)'. مر او را is an oblique dative/topic cluster: مر (an archaic/to‑me‑type clitic that here participates in a dative/indirect‑object construction) plus او را ('him' with object marker) — together indicating 'to him' / 'for him'. چو here is simile 'like/as'. رومی پرند is a nominal phrase 'Roman birds' (پرند = birds/winged ones); the final image is 'the world came to him / filled him as Roman birds (i.e. like a flight of Romans)'.
segments.s547-g3: ز هر مرز = 'from every border/frontier' (z + determiner + noun). پیوسته شد is the past/inchoative 'became continual / kept arising'. باژ و ساو are nouns (onomatopoetic or names) functioning as the things that arose: 'cries/noises and (saav)'; the exact lexical sense of ساو is uncertain/poetic here.
segments.s547-g4: کسی را نبد is an archaic negative-existential: نبد (negated past of 'to be') with object-marked کسی را yields 'there was no one'. با here works 'with / compared to' introducing the standard of comparison; جهاندار تاو = 'the world's-ruler's radiance' (جهاندار = sovereign, تاو = radiance/glow). Overall: 'there was no one who compared, with the sovereign's radiance.'
segments.s547-g5: همه مهتران را = 'all the chiefs/elders' with را marking the definite/direct object. ز ایران = 'from Iran' (source). بخواند is simple past 'he called/summoned' (3rd person singular). Word order: object before the verb is common in classical Persian.
segments.s547-g6: سزاوار functions as a substantive/adjective 'the deserving/fit one'. بر تخت شاهی = 'upon the throne of kingship' (بر = on/upon, تخت = throne, شاهی = kingship). نشاند is an archaic causative/past 'he set/caused to sit' (i.e. 'he seated [him]'). The whole clause describes the act of seating the deserving person on the royal throne.
segments.s547-g7: ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'after that / thereafter'. شهنشاه is the subject 'the king of kings / emperor'. بر پای خاست is an idiomatic verb phrase 'stood up' (literally 'rose upon his feet'); خاست is the simple past 'rose'.
segments.s547-g8: به خوبی is an adverbial phrase 'well / fittingly' (به here forms an adverbial with خوبی). بیاراست is a past verb 'he adorned / made beautiful' (here applied to speech). گفتار راست = 'true speech' or 'a truthful utterance' (noun phrase). Together: 'he composed/ delivered a well‑adorned, truthful speech.'
segments.s547-g65: This clause lists possessions: نهالی (sapling) begins the line as a topical/poetic noun, then همه (everyone) is the plural quantifier/subject and دارند is the present plural verb 'have'. The possessed items follow (خاک و خشت), with و linking خاک and خشت; word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect (topic noun before subject).
segments.s547-g66: خنک is an adjective that belongs with خشت in the previous segment ('cool mudbrick'). آنک is a relative/indefinite pronoun 'he who/that', جز is the preposition 'except', تخم نیکی is the noun phrase 'seed of goodness', and نکشت here functions as an archaic past negative verb 'did not sow/plant' (classical negative past formed with ن‑ + past stem), the main verb of this clause referring back to آنک.
segments.s547-g67: همه + هرک ('هر که' = 'whoever') form a generalizing subject 'everyone who/whatever person'. هست is the copula/existential 'is', and اندرین مرز من is the locative phrase 'in this border/realm of mine' (اندرین = 'in these/this'). The phrase order is compressed for verse but means 'everyone who is in this my realm'.
segments.s547-g68: This is a rhetorical question: کجا 'where' introduces it; گوش دارند literally 'have ears' (گوش = ear, here figuratively 'attention, willingness to listen'), دارند is present plural agreeing with the preceding general subject, and اندرز من 'my advice' is the object (اندرز = counsel, من = my).
segments.s547-g69: نمایم is first-person singular present/subjunctive used like a future 'I will show' or 'let me show'; شما را is the marked object ('you' with the object marker را), کنون is the adverb 'now', and راه پنج is the noun + numeral 'the five ways' (poetic placement of the number after the noun).
segments.s547-g70: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'راه پنج' ('which/whose'); سودش is 'its profit' (سود + enclitic ـش), فزون آید is a verbal phrase 'comes/grows greater' (فزون 'more/increasing' + آید 'comes'), and از تاج و گنج marks the comparison 'than crown and treasure' using از. The verb آید is third-person singular agreeing with سودش.
segments.s547-g41: کنون هرچ خواهیم کردن ز داد = 'Now, whatever we wish to do by/through justice.' کنون is an adverb ('now'). هرچ + خواهیم کردن is a relative/indefinite clause: هرچ (’whatever’) governs خواهیم (present-subjunctive/attitudinal form of 'to want') plus کردن (the light verb 'to do') so together 'whatever we want to do.' ز is the poetic preposition (from/by), modifying داد ('justice'). Word order is topic/adverb — action phrase then the adjunct 'ز داد'.
segments.s547-g42: بکوشیم وز داد باشیم شاد = 'let us strive so that, through justice, we may be happy.' بکوشیم is 1st-person-plural hortatory/subjunctive meaning 'let us strive/endeavour.' وز here functions as a prepositional/archaic variant of 'از' ('by/through'); it links to داد ('justice'), which is the means. باشیم is the 1st-plural subjunctive of 'to be' (hortatory: 'let us be'); شاد is predicate adjective 'happy.'
segments.s547-g43: ز ده یک مرا چند بر شهرهاست = a partitive/quantitative phrase plus a question. ز ده یک = 'one of ten' (partitive). مرا is the speaker-related form ('of mine' / 'to me') split in the provided tokenization (م + را); چند is an interrogative 'how many'; بر شهرهاست is a locative comment 'upon/in the towns are' (بر + شهرها + است). The overall word order places the partitive first, then the possessor/affected phrase, then the question word and the locative predicate.
segments.s547-g44: که دهقان و موبد بران بر گواست = a relative clause introduced by که ('where/that') describing those towns. دهقان و موبد are nouns in coordination 'the dehqān (landed gentry) and the mōbed (Zoroastrian priest)'. بران بر is a doubled locative/covering phrase ('upon them / over them'); گواست here functions as a predicate in the clause (the exact lexical sense in the supplied list is uncertain, but syntactically it is the verb/predicate of the relative clause).
segments.s547-g45: چو باید شما را ببخشم همه = 'If/when it is right/necessary that I grant to you all.' چو here introduces a conditional/temporal ('if'/'when'); باید is the impersonal/necessitative 'must/it is necessary'; شما را is the object ('to you'); ببخشم is 1st-singular subjunctive/optative of بخشیدن used here in the sense 'to give/bestow' (not 'forgive'); همه attaches to indicate 'all/everything.'
segments.s547-g46: همان ده یک و بوم و باژ و رمه = the list of items to be given. همان ('the same' / 'those very') points back to what was spoken of; ده یک = 'one of ten' (a fixed fraction/tithe); بوم = 'land'/'homestead'; باژ here is a term in the poem (context suggests a kind of due/tribute or levy — the supplied senses do not reflect that meaning exactly); رمه = 'herds/flocks.' The coordination is a simple enumerative list.
segments.s547-g47: مگر آنک آید شما را فزون = 'except/unless that which comes brings you more.' مگر is the conditional/exceptive particle ('unless'/'except'); آنک ('that which') introduces the subordinate idea; آید is the present/subjunctive of 'to come' (here 'should come' / 'happen'); شما را marks 'you' as the affected party; فزون is an adjective/adverb 'more/greater' indicating increased benefit.
segments.s547-g48: بیارد سوی گنج ما رهنمون = 'may bring (you) toward our treasure as a guide.' بیارد here is the verb meaning 'bring/produce' (the supplied lexical senses in the list do not match the poetic verbal form; syntactically it is a third-person-subjunctive/optative verb 'may bring'). سوی = 'toward'; گنج = 'treasure'; ما = 'our'; رهنمون = 'guide/guidance' (here the thing that leads one toward the treasure).
segments.s547-g49: ز ده یک = a prepositional phrase: ز (poetic 'from') + ده یک ('one‑tenth'). The relative clause که من بستدم پیش ازین follows: که 'which', من 'I' (subject), بستدم is past tense, 1st person singular (classical/persian past: 'I bound/set aside'), and پیش ازین is an adverbial phrase ('before this / previously') composed of پیش + از + ین. Word order here is noun/phrase + relative clause + verb, a common classical Persian arrangement.
segments.s547-g50: ز باژ آنچ کم بود‌گر بیش ازین is built around two prepositional/conditional pieces. ز باژ 'from the cry/voice' (ز = 'from'), آنچ 'that which' modifies کم 'little' (i.e. 'that little which there was'); بودگر is the conditional/optative compound (بود + گر) meaning 'if it were' or 'were it', and بیش ازین = 'more than this' (بیش 'more' + از + ین). The line uses an archaic fused form بودگر for the protasis ('if it were').
segments.s547-g51: همی از پی سود بردم is a classical clause: همی is a durative adverb (marks continuous or habitual action in past), از پی 'in pursuit of/after' + سود 'profit' is the object, and بردم is past tense 1st person singular of بردن used here in the sense 'to gain / obtain'. The verb follows the object phrase, as is typical in older Persian.
segments.s547-g52: به در داشتن لشکر بی‌شمار is an infinitival/nominal purpose phrase: به در داشتن literally 'to have/keep at hand' (به = 'for/to', در = 'in/at (read as 'ready')', داشتن = 'to have'), followed by its object لشکر بی‌شمار 'an army countless/innumerable' (لشکر 'army', بی‑شمار 'countless'). The whole expresses purpose/result: seeking profit in order to maintain a huge army.
segments.s547-g53: بزرگی شما جستم puts the possessed noun 'بزرگی شما' ('your greatness') before the verb جستم (past 1st person of جستن). Here جستم means 'I sought' (classical meaning 'to seek'). The coordinating و then begins the next nominal 'ایمنی' ('safety/security'), so the speaker lists what he sought: your greatness and safety.
segments.s547-g54: نهان کردن کیش آهرمنی is a nominalized infinitive phrase: نهان + کردن ('to hide, to conceal') forming 'the hiding/concealment of' and followed by its object کیش آهرمنی ('the Ahrimanic faith/sect'), where کیش = 'creed/sect' and آهرمنی = 'Ahrimanic/demonic'. Grammatically this phrase explains the kind of 'ایمنی' named before (i.e. safety by concealing that faith).
segments.s547-g55: شما دست یکسر به یزدان زنید is an address with an imperative/subjunctive verb. شما 'you' is the addressee, دست 'hand' is used figuratively (a pledge/commitment), یکسر 'entirely/wholly' is an adverb modifying the action, به یزدان 'to God', and زنید is the 2nd person plural subjunctive/imperative of زدن ('to put/strike/offer'), here functioning as a command: 'place/offer your whole hand to God' (i.e. pledge yourselves wholly to God).
segments.s547-g56: بکوشید و پیمان او مشکنید contains two coordinated imperatives: بکوشید ('make an effort / strive') is 2nd person plural imperative/subjunctive of کوشیدن; و = 'and'; پیمان او 'his covenant' (او as 3rd‑person pronoun used possessively) is the object of the negative imperative مشکنید ('do not break' — present/subjunctive negative of شکستن used here as a prohibition). The sequence gives commands: strive and do not violate His covenant.
segments.s547-g57: که here introduces a relative clause ('who'/'that'). بخشنده is a predicate noun/adjective meaning 'generous' used as an identifying attribute. اویست is an archaic fused form acting as a copula ('he is' = او + است), and و links the coordinated predicate دارنده (possessor). اوی at the end is the pronominal subject 'he' that identifies the owner; the clause as a whole says 'who is generous and is the possessor (he)'.
segments.s547-g58: بلند 'lofty/high' modifies آسمان 'the sky/heaven'. را is the classical definite/object marker attached after آسمان (topicalizing/marking the definite object). نگارنده is an agent noun 'painter/one who fashions or inscribes' and اوی is the pronominal subject 'he' (so: 'he who is the painter/creator of the lofty sky'). Word order is poetic: adjective + object marked by را + agent + pronoun.
segments.s547-g59: ستمدیده 'the oppressed/afflicted' is topicalized and followed by را, the definite/accusative marker. اوست is the copula (او + ست/است) 'he is', and فریادرس is the predicate noun 'rescuer/succor' — together: 'he is the rescuer of the afflicted.'
segments.s547-g60: منازید here is a jussive/imperative-form verb meaning 'contend/strive (you plural)'; in the poem it functions as a prohibition in context ('do not vie'). با is the preposition 'with/by'. نازش is ناز + ـش (naaz + possessive suffix) meaning 'his favor/affection'; ش is the possessive suffix 'his'. او following repeats/identifies the possessor ('he'); به کس is a prepositional phrase 'toward/for anyone' (به = to/for, کس = anyone/person).
segments.s547-g61: نباید is an impersonal prohibition ('one must not' / 'should not'). It takes the infinitive نهادن 'to put/place' (here nominal infinitive) and the object دل 'the heart'. اندر is the classical preposition 'in/within'. فریب is the noun 'deception/entrapment' — together: 'one must not put the heart into deception.'
segments.s547-g62: که introduces an explanatory clause ('for'/'because'/'that'). پیش فراز = 'before/preceding the height/top' (پیش = before, فراز = summit/height). اندر آید is a verbal phrase 'comes into/occurs' (اندر classical preposition + آید 'comes'), and نشیب 'descent/fall' is placed sentence-final (poetic order). The clause means roughly 'for after an ascent, a descent comes' (i.e. a fall follows the rise).
segments.s547-g63: کجا آنک is a rhetorical formula 'where is he who/where is the one that...' (کجا = where; آنک = 'that/who'). بر … به ابر expresses reaching up to the clouds: بر here 'on/upon', تاجش 'his crown' (تاج + ـش possessive). سود in classical poetic usage here colors the phrase poetically (literal senses include 'profit/benefit', but contextually it helps denote the crown's summit/edge). به ابر 'to the cloud(s)' indicates the crown's height touching the clouds.
segments.s547-g64: Again کجا آنک begins the rhetorical question 'where is he who...'. بودی is the past form 'was' (you/he was in older forms; here 'who used to be'). شکارش is شکار + ـش 'his prey/quarry' (shakār = prey), and هژبر is a proper name (Hojabr) — 'where is he whose quarry was Hojabr?' The order is poetic: verb (was) precedes the noun (prey).
segments.s547-g17: به گیتی is a locative prepositional phrase (literally 'in/at the world'); ممانید is an archaic negative present/continuous verb 'does not remain' (third-person sense) placed after the locative phrase. جز نام نیک is the exception phrase ('except a good name') that follows the verb and completes the clause.
segments.s547-g18: هرانکس که is an indefinite relative ('whoever, whoever... who'); خواهد is third-person present ('wishes/wants'); سرانجام نیک is the noun phrase 'a good outcome/end' that functions as the object/complement of خواهد. The relative clause attaches to the preceding clause to specify for whom the exception (a good name) holds.
segments.s547-g19: ترا is split here into the clitic ت (second-person object) plus the object marker را; together they mark 'for you'/'to you' as the beneficiary. روزگار اورمزد آن بود is a copular construction: روزگار (fate/time) + اورمزد (Ormazd, the deity) + آن بود ('it was that'), where آن is a demonstrative/anticipatory element introducing the content clause that follows.
segments.s547-g20: که introduces the content clause ('that ...'); خشنودی پاک یزدان بود is a nominal predicate: خشنودی 'approval/pleasure' modified by پاک 'pure' and followed by یزدان 'God' (genitive relation: 'the pure God's approval'), closed by the copula بود ('was' / 'should be' in sense of ordained).
segments.s547-g21: This line gives two imperatives coordinated by و: به یزدان گرای and به یزدان گشای. به یزدان is the prepositional complement 'to God / toward God.' گرای and گشای are verbal imperatives/jussives meaning 'turn (to)' and 'open (to)' respectively; they are commands addressed to the addressee.
segments.s547-g22: که introduces an explanatory clause ('for / because') giving the reason. دارنده is a predicate noun 'holder/possessor'; اویست here is an archaic fused form (literally 'او یست' = 'he is'), serving as the copula. و links the second predicate نیکی فزای, where نیکی 'goodness' is the object and فزای is a volitive/present form meaning roughly 'increases' or 'may increase'—i.e. 'He is the Holder and (He) increases goodness.'
segments.s547-g23: ز هر بد is a prepositional phrase 'from every evil' (ز = 'from'); به دادار گیهان پناه is a verbal-nominal construction meaning 'seek refuge with the Creator of the world' where به + [proper name/title] + پناه expresses 'take refuge in/with X.' دادار گیهان is a genitival/name phrase 'Creator of the world.'
segments.s547-g24: که introduces the explanatory clause 'for / because.' او راست بر frames the predicate 'he is just/right regarding ...' where بر marks relation/target ('with respect to' or 'in the dispensation of'). نیک و بد دستگاه is the noun phrase 'the ordering/dispensation of good and evil' (دستگاه = system/dispensation). The whole clause states God's just manner with respect to good and bad.
segments.s547-g33: همی is an emphatic/habitual marker (classical/poetic) meaning something like “continually” or “I will (ever)”. باژ is a noun used here verbally as “cry/outcry.” خواهم is the 1st‑person singular present/future auxiliary (“I will” / “I desire to”), so the clause reads literally “I will cry…”; ز + place names is the poetic preposition “from.” Word order is subject (implied “I”) + adverbial + verb + prepositional phrases.
segments.s547-g34: This clause uses شد (past of شدن) in the sense “became” with مرا functioning as the dative/object pronoun (“to me” / “mine”) split orthographically into م + را; همچو = “like.” The phrase arranges predicate before its simile: “the world became (to) me like a Roman bird.” پرند is a poetic noun “bird/winged one.” The overall structure is topical (جهان) + verb (شد) + indirect object (مرا) + simile (همچو ...).
segments.s547-g35: سپاسم is a nominalized or verblike expression “my/ I give thanks” (classical diction). ز Yezdân = “to/for God” (the poetic preposition ز here). که introduces a relative/explanatory clause “who/for he” and او داد زور is a simple past clause “he gave strength.” داد is past “gave” rather than the noun “justice” in this context.
segments.s547-g36: This is a nominal list of gifts: بلند اختر = “a lofty/high star” (بلند used adjectivally), and بخش is used in the sense “portion/gift/grace.” کیوان and هور are cosmic names/terms (planet/astrological names or personified lights); و simply links items. The line is a coordination of nouns describing what was given.
segments.s547-g37: ستایش is a nominative/exclamatory noun “praise.” که introduces a relative clause “who/that,” and داند is the present 3rd‑person singular “knows.” سزاوار اوی literally means “what is fitting of him / due to him” (سزاوار + pronominal اوی). The clause is elliptical: “Praise (belongs) to Him who knows what is fitting.”
segments.s547-g38: نیایش is “prayer/worship.” بر here has the sense “according to / in accordance with” (classical use). آیین و کردار اوی = “His ordinance/law and His conduct/deeds,” with اوی the pronominal suffix referring back to God. The sequence prescribes the proper manner of worship: “worship according to His law and deeds.”
segments.s547-g39: مگر here introduces a rhetorical/exclusive question “who else but… / except.” کو is an archaic interrogative/relative particle (classical Persian “who/where is”) and pairs with مگر to ask “who (is there) except (Him)?” دهد is the present/subjunctive/optative sense “would give/grant” and بازمان زندگی = “a remnant/surviving life” (بازمان = remaining; زندگی = life). The clause is rhetorical, asking who can bestow surviving life.
segments.s547-g40: بماند is a present/subjunctive/optative form meaning “remain/last/endure.” بزرگی و تابندگی are coordinated nouns “greatness and radiance” (the things whose continuance is being asked about). The verb governs the coordinated objects: “so that greatness and radiance remain.”
segments.s547-g25: کند is a third-person present-form verb (here optative/jussive: 'may/makes'), meaning 'make/do' (sense 0). The phrase بر تو ('for/unto you') follows the verb; آسان is the predicate adjective 'easy' that governs the following noun phrase همه کار سخت ('all hard work'), where همه quantifies کار and سخت modifies کار. Literal order: 'makes for you easy all hard work.'
segments.s547-g26: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/by' (sense 0) introducing the instrumental/causal phrase. رای here means 'plan/counsel' (sense 1), and دلفروز and پیروز are epithets describing بخت ('fortune') — دلفروز 'heart‑illuminating' (sense 0) and پیروز 'victorious' (sense 0); بخت is 'fate/fortune' (sense 1). Together the phrase means 'by the counsel/agency of the heart‑illuminating and victorious fortune.'
segments.s547-g27: نخستین 'first' (sense 0) is an adverbial / ordering word. ز کار من = 'from/of my affairs' (ز = from, کار = deeds, من functions here as possessive 'my' though lemma is 'I'). اندازه گیر is a verbal phrase: اندازه ('measure/extent') + گیر (verb 'take/seize', sense 1) giving the imperative 'take measure of / consider.'
segments.s547-g28: This is a continuation of the command: گذشته بد و نیک من = 'my past, bad and good' (گذشته 'past' modifies 'بد و نیک من'), and تازه گیر is an imperative 'take as new' or 'accept anew' where تازه 'fresh/new' modifies the verbal گیر (sense 1). Word order places modifiers before the verb in compact poetic form.
segments.s547-g29: که here introduces the subordinate clause ('that/which' or 'for/because' depending on context; sense 0 taken as 'that/which'). کردم is first‑person past 'I did/sought' (sense 0). به دادار گیهان پناه is a prepositional phrase 'to/with the Creator of the world (دادار گیهان) [for] refuge (پناه)', so the clause means 'that I sought refuge with the Creator of the world.'
segments.s547-g30: مرا داد is 'gave me' with مرا (object 'me') split in the token list; the token 'م' as listed does not match its use here (so sense set is not applicable → -1). را is the object marker (sense 0), داد is 'gave' (sense 1). بر introduces the beneficiary/extent 'over' (sense 1), and نیک و بد دستگاه means 'the affairs/arrangement (دستگاه) of good and bad' — i.e. 'matters of good and evil.' Together: 'he gave me authority/jurisdiction over good and evil matters.'
segments.s547-g31: زمین هفت کشور is a fixed poetic phrase 'the earth of seven countries/climes' (زمین sense 0, کشور sense 0). به شاهی = 'to/for kingship' (به sense 0; شاهی here 'kingship', sense 2). مراست is a fused poetic form meaning 'is mine' (literally مرا + است); the token list gave unrelated senses, so the provided senses for مراست do not apply (→ -1). The clause says these lands belong to me by virtue of my kingship.
segments.s547-g32: چنان 'so/such' (sense 0) introduces the result/explanatory clause. کز is the contracted form 'که از' meaning 'that/from which' (sense 0). خداوندی 'lordship' (sense 0) + او 'his/he' (sense 0) and سزاست 'it is fitting/it is deserved' (sense 0) together mean 'such, as is fitting from His lordship' (i.e. it is appropriate given God's lordship).

sentences.s547-s5: Thus he said: “O notables of the city, whoever among you has any counsel or wisdom to offer...”
sentences.s547-s6: Know that this arrow‑turner of the heavens neither esteems justice nor needs affection.
sentences.s547-s7: When it pleases him he lifts someone high, and in the end consigns him to base dust.
sentences.s547-s8: Nothing remains of him in the world but his name; all his suffering will befall him in secret.
sentences.s547-s1: When people came from Rome and from China and from the Turks and India, the world came to him like Roman birds.
sentences.s547-s2: From every border there rose continual cries and exclamations, and there was no one to compare with the sovereign in that radiance.
sentences.s547-s3: He summoned all the chiefs from Iran and set the worthy upon the throne of kingship.
sentences.s547-s4: After that the king of kings rose to his feet and made a well‑fitting, truthful speech.
sentences.s547-s33: Everyone has a sapling, soil and a cool mudbrick — except him who planted nothing but the seed of goodness.
sentences.s547-s34: All — whoever is within my border — where do they have ears for my counsel?
sentences.s547-s35: I will now show you the five ways whose profit exceeds crown and treasure.
sentences.s547-s21: Now, whatever we wish to do, let us strive to do it by justice and be glad.
sentences.s547-s22: Of ten, how many of mine are in the towns where the dehqān and the mōbed are present (as officials/witnesses)?
sentences.s547-s23: If I should have to give you everything, I will give you the same: one-tenth, the land, the tribute, and the flocks.
sentences.s547-s24: Unless something should come that brings you more and guides you toward our treasure.
sentences.s547-s25: From the one‑tenth that I had set aside before now — from that little of the outcry (that there was), if it were more than this...
sentences.s547-s26: I continually pursued profit, for the purpose of keeping an innumerable army.
sentences.s547-s27: I sought your greatness and the security of concealing the Ahrimanic faith.
sentences.s547-s28: Give yourselves wholly to God; strive, and do not break His covenant.
sentences.s547-s29: He is the Generous and the Possessor — he who fashioned the high heaven.
sentences.s547-s30: He is the helper of the oppressed; do not contend with anyone for his favor.
sentences.s547-s31: One must not place the heart in deception, for after a rise a fall comes.
sentences.s547-s32: Where is he whose crown reached the clouds? Where is he for whom Hojabr was his quarry?
sentences.s547-s9: Nothing remains in the world except a good name for whoever wishes a good outcome.
sentences.s547-s10: Ormazd had decreed for you that the pure God's approval should be (with you).
sentences.s547-s11: Turn to God and open (yourself) to God, for He is the Holder and increases goodness.
sentences.s547-s12: From every evil take refuge in the Creator of the world, for He is just in His dispensation of good and bad.
sentences.s547-s17: I will cry out from Rûm and from Hind; the world has become to me like a Roman bird.
sentences.s547-s18: I give thanks to God, for He gave mighty strength, a lofty star, and the gift/grace of Kayvân and Hôr.
sentences.s547-s19: Praise be to Him who knows what is fitting; let worship be according to His ordinance and conduct.
sentences.s547-s20: Who else but He can grant remaining life, so that greatness and radiance endure?
sentences.s547-s13: May the heart-illumining and victorious fortune make all your hard tasks easy.
sentences.s547-s14: First, take measure of my deeds: accept my past, bad and good, and receive my present as new.
sentences.s547-s15: For I sought refuge with the Creator of the world; he gave me authority over matters of good and evil.
sentences.s547-s16: The earth of the seven climes is mine to rule as king, for such is fitting from His lordship.
